entry.title: Re: DMO: background on table split mechanism
entry.content:
Hi  Bilgekagan

is this parameter applicable for DMO with system Move case?

  • /clonepar/maxprocpertable = 100

Assume you have configured 500 R3load processes and some large tables are split each in 200 segments. When the transfer starts, 400 processes are working on the first table only (200 export + 200 import). Only when these buckets gradually finish will other tables be picked up.

By setting the above parameter, at most 100 processes are simultaneously active for each table (50 export + 50 imports), so more tables are loaded in parallel.

Please note that if no other buckets are to be loaded again, this parameter still affects how many processes are running per table! So if there are only buckets left for one table and 500 processes are configured, at most 100 will be running

https://me.sap.com/notes/3386079

 

Akash Ahuja

entry.title: Publish RFQ from custom behavior class save method
entry.content:
Dear Experts,

I have a requirement to develop custom application in HANA Public Cloud where user selects specific supplier to approve then it should automatically publish RFQ. Lo_http_client being generated but session getting terminated at lo_http_client->execute method and not catching at exceptions. Request your valuable inputs to take it forward. below is the sample code for your reference. Thank you
 
lo_url = 'https://{hostname}/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_RFQ_PROCESS_SRV/SubmitForApproval?RequestForQuotation='7000000010''

lo_http_client = cl_web_http_client_manager=>create_by_http_destination(

i_destination = cl_http_destination_provider=>create_by_url( lo_url ) ).
Try

DATA(lo_response) = lo_http_client->execute(

i_method = if_web_http_client=>post ).

CATCH cx_web_message_error cx_web_http_client_error cx_http_dest_provider_error. "#EC NO_HANDLER

" data(lv_msg) = lo_Response->get_text( ).

ENDTRY.

entry.title: Access Error in HANA CV - DBTech JDBC: [258]: insufficient privilege: [2950] user is not authorized
entry.content:
Hi All, while working with security around HANA CVs, I faced the below error multiple times while retrieving data from the CV, it took a while to understand the root cause

Error: SAP DBTech JDBC: [258]: insufficient privilege: [2950] user is not authorized :

Even though the CV had Apply Privilege check maintained as Blank in View Properties, we got this error -

To understand more about the access errors while accessing data from CV, there are mainly two -

1. When we see the GUID in error -

This means the Analytic Privilege is being checked for the CV which you are previewing the data from.

Two ways to resolve this -

  •    Either create a analytic priv (SQL/Classic accordingly) and it to the user profile directly or through role.
  • Or if not needed, mark apply privilege as blank in the view properties of CV.

2. When we see a generic error -

User faces this error when one of the underlying CVs (Z or standard ones generated through Data Modeling) has the Apply Privilege Check enabled. you can check all the views used in Scenario section of the CV. For example in this case, as you can see, there was only one, disable the check at the individual CV level (or enable the check and add it to the analytic priv). Just to add, there can be multiple Views being used in a CV.

You can hover over the nodes to see, in which package the CV is placed -

Objects created through data modeling like composite providers, BW characteristics are inside the package -

Few important points to note here - 

  • In the latest HANA versions, the default check applied on CVs is SQL Analytic Privileges.
  • Even if you mark Apply Priv check as Blank for BW objects like Comp Provider etc. If regenerated, in modeling Perspective, the check will be applied again.
  • SYS_BI_CP_ALL (default SAP_ALL priv for CVs) only work for CVs when the Apply priv check is set as Classic Analytic Priv.

Please comment if there are any questions on handling the security for HAAN Objects. thank you.

entry.title: How Can You Simplify SAP Service Cloud CTI Integration Testing? Try This Dynamic POC Widget!
entry.content:
Introduction

When integrating Computer Telephony Integration (CTI) with SAP Service Cloud Agent Desktop, ensuring that the required payloads are constructed and processed correctly is a critical step. This blog intents to walk you through creating a  Proof of Concept (POC) widget designed to provide a clear and interactive way to validate payload structures and test the seamless integration of various common CTI scenarios.

It’s important to note that it is the responsibility of the customer and CTI integrators to create their own widgets and ensure that the correct payloads are sent to the parent window using the window.parent.postMessage() method. This is in line with the guidance provided in the Widget Integration SAP Help Online Documentation.

This approach applies universally—whether you are working with the Agent Desktop Add-On or the Stand-Alone Agent Desktop in Service Cloud V2, as well as with Live Activity in SAP Customer Cloud (C4C).

To System Integrators, we recommend considering the SAP CTI Certification Process to ensure compliance and compatibility. Would you like to know more about the SAP Certification Process? Please check out the Knowledge Base Article KBA 3307629 for detailed information.

The primary expectation is that CTI integrators will use this POC to understand the required parameters and payload formats, ultimately ensuring they meet the integration requirements. This POC demonstrates what payloads are expected, how they can be constructed, and how they integrate with SAP Service Cloud Agent Desktop.

By the end of this blog, you'll gain insights into:

  • How to construct payloads for interactions like calls, chats, and messages.
  • How to push these payloads during integration testing with SAP Service Cloud Agent Desktop.
  • How to extend this POC for additional use cases, including Live Activity.

Key Features of the POC

  1. Support for Multiple Interaction Types:

    • Select the different variables to start building the payload.
    • Handles CALL, CHAT, and MESSAGE interaction types.
      • EventTypes like INBOUND, OUTBOUND, UPDATEACTIVITY, and TRANSFER.
      • Actions like Notify, Accept and END

  2. Easy Payload Customization:

    • XML payloads are automatically generated based on user inputs.
    • Fields like ANI, Email, Transcript, ExternalReferenceID and custom fields are included dynamically.
    • Option to Generate Random GUID (max of 35 characters, no hyphens or spaces and all in UPPERCASE)
    • Copy to Call Recording from previously generated GUID. This is typically the same when generated by many CTI providers.

  3. Real-time Validation:

    • Review payloads in real-time before sending them to the parent window.

  4. Extensible Design:

    • Easily add new fields or extend the payload structure for more complex use cases.

Using the POC





 

 

 

 

 




Interaction Types

The widget provides fields for all interactions (CALL, CHAT, and MESSAGE), and all fields are displayed regardless of the selected interaction type. While not all fields are relevant for every interaction, you can selectively populate the fields needed for your scenario.

Event Types

  • INBOUND: For incoming interactions.
  • OUTBOUND: For outgoing interactions.
  • UPDATEACTIVITY: Used to update existing activities.
  • TRANSFER: Handles interaction transfers between agents.

Example Payloads

The payloads below follow the format and structure outlined in the SAP Help Portal Widget Integration Guide. These examples demonstrate how the widget generates XML payloads for common CTI interaction scenarios.

Phone Call Payloads

1. Notify (Inbound Call):
The NOTIFY action informs the system of an incoming phone call.

 

<payload>
    <Type>CALL</Type>
    <EventType>INBOUND</EventType>
    <Action>NOTIFY</Action>
    <ANI>+12345678910</ANI>
    <ExternalReferenceID>ED4E4730D1C711EAAA5EBC019352B05E</ExternalReferenceID>
</payload>

 

2. Accept (Call Accepted by Agent):
The ACCEPT action indicates that the agent has accepted the call. Note that in some cases, when sending the the Action = ACCEPT does not work, then send action with null value. In the below example, the <Action> tag is left empty.

 

<payload>
    <Type>CALL</Type>
    <EventType>INBOUND</EventType>
    <Action></Action>
    <ANI>+12345678910</ANI>
    <ExternalReferenceID>ED4E4730D1C711EAAA5EBC019352B05E</ExternalReferenceID>
</payload>

 

3. End (Call Concluded):
The END action marks the conclusion of the phone interaction.

 

<payload>
    <Type>CALL</Type>
    <EventType>UPDATEACTIVITY</EventType>
    <Action>END</Action>
    <ANI>+12345678910</ANI>
    <ExternalReferenceID>ED4E4730D1C711EAAA5EBC019352B05E</ExternalReferenceID>
    <RecordingId>REC_1234567890</RecordingId>
</payload>

 

4. Transfer (Call Transferred to Another Agent):
The TRANSFER event indicates that the call has been transferred.

 

<payload>
    <Type>CALL</Type>
    <EventType>TRANSFER</EventType>
    <Action>NOTIFY</Action>
    <ANI>+12345678910</ANI>
    <ExternalReferenceID>ED4E4730D1C711EAAA5EBC019352B05E</ExternalReferenceID>
 <ExternalOriginalReferenceID>ED4E4730D1C711EAAA5EBC019352B05F</ExternalOriginalReferenceID>
</payload>

 

Chat Payloads

1. Notify (New Chat Interaction):
The NOTIFY action notifies the system of a new chat interaction.

 

<payload>
    <Type>CHAT</Type>
    <EventType>INBOUND</EventType>
    <Action>NOTIFY</Action>
    <Email>Road.Runner@test.com</Email>
    <ExternalReferenceID>ED4E4730D1C711EAAA5EBC019352B05E</ExternalReferenceID>
</payload>​

 

2. Accept (Chat Interaction Accepted):
The ACCEPT action indicates that the chat has been accepted by the agent.

 

<payload>
    <Type>CHAT</Type>
    <EventType>INBOUND</EventType>
    <Action></Action>
    <Email>Road.Runner@test.com</Email>
    <ExternalReferenceID>ED4E4730D1C711EAAA5EBC019352B05E</ExternalReferenceID>
</payload>

 

3. End (Chat Interaction Concluded):
The END action uploads the transcript and concludes the chat interaction.

 

<payload>
    <Type>CHAT</Type>
    <EventType>UPDATEACTIVITY</EventType>
    <Action>END</Action>
    <Email>mike.ross@test.com</Email>
    <ExternalReferenceID>ED4E4730D1C711EAAA5EBC019352B05E</ExternalReferenceID>
    <Transcript>
        support@sap.com: Hello Mike
        Road.Runner@test.com: Hello
        Road.Runner@test.com: I need to book an appointment for fixing my washing machine
        support@sap.com: Sure, will tomorrow 12.00 PM work for you?
        Road.Runner@test.com: Yes, thanks! Bye.
        support@sap.com: Bye.
    </Transcript>
</payload>

 

Message Payloads

1. Notify (New Message Interaction):
The NOTIFY action indicates that a new message has been received.

 

<payload>
    <Type>MESSAGE</Type>
    <EventType>INBOUND</EventType>
    <Action>NOTIFY</Action>
    <ANI>+12345678910</ANI>
    <Text>This is SMS text</Text>
    <ExternalReferenceID>77FB2990D24411EAAA5EBC019352B05E</ExternalReferenceID>
</payload>

 

2. Accept (SMS Acknowledged):
The ACCEPT action indicates that the SMS message has been acknowledged by the agent. Note that the <Action> tag is left empty.

 

<payload>
    <Type>MESSAGE</Type>
    <EventType>INBOUND</EventType>
    <Action></Action>
    <ANI>+12345678910</ANI>
    <Text>This is SMS text</Text>
    <ExternalReferenceID>77FB2990D24411EAAA5EBC019352B05E</ExternalReferenceID>
</payload>

 

3. End (SMS Interaction Concluded):
The END action marks the conclusion of the SMS interaction.

 

<payload>
    <Type>MESSAGE</Type>
    <EventType>UPDATEACTIVITY</EventType>
    <Action>END</Action>
    <ANI>+12345678910</ANI>
    <Text>Thank you for your assistance!</Text>
    <ExternalReferenceID>77FB2990D24411EAAA5EBC019352B05E</ExternalReferenceID>
</payload>

 

Preparing the Widget for Use

Before configuring the widget in SAP Service Cloud or SAP Customer Cloud (C4C), you need to ensure that the widget files (index.html, style.css, and script.js) are hosted on a web server that supports HTML hosting.

Step 1: Upload the Widget Files to a Web Server

  1. Choose a web server that supports hosting HTML, CSS, and JavaScript files. Examples include:

    • Apache
    • Nginx
    • IIS (Windows Server)
    • Any cloud-based static hosting service such as AWS S3, Azure Blob Storage, or Netlify.

  2. Upload the following files to the server:

    • index.html
    • style.css
    • script.js

  3. Ensure that the files are publicly accessible via a URL. For example:

    • https://yourserver.com/path-to-widget/index.html

  4. Test the widget by opening the URL in a browser to confirm that it loads correctly.

Once the files are hosted and accessible, proceed with the following configuration steps. The hosting step applies to all scenarios below.

Scenario 1: Agent Desktop in Service Cloud V2 

  1. Host the Widget Files:

    • Use the steps outlined above to ensure the files are hosted and accessible.

  2. Update the Content Security Policy (CSP):

    • Add the hosted widget's URL to the Content Security Policy (CSP) to avoid security-related issues.
    • Refer to SAP KBA 3371582 - Adding URLs to the Content Security Policy for SAP Service Cloud V2 for detailed instructions.

  3. Configure in SAP Service Cloud V2:

    • Navigate to Administrator > General Settings > CTI Settings.
    • Enter the URL of the hosted widget in the Agent Desktop URL field.

  4. Test the Integration:

    • Open the Agent Desktop in Service Cloud V2 and interact with the widget.
    • Validate that payloads are sent and received correctly.

Scenario 2: Agent Desktop Add-On in C4C

  1. Host the Widget Files:

    • Follow the hosting steps outlined above.

  2. Configure in SAP Customer Cloud (C4C):

    1. Access Agent Desktop Configuration:

      • Navigate to Administrator > Service and Social > Configure Agent Desktop.
      • Refer to Configure communication system provider .

    2. Select Widget and Provider Configuration:

      • In the Configure Agent Desktop settings, click on Configure Widget and Provider.

    3. Enter Provider Details:

      • Provider Name: Enter the name of your communication provider (e.g., "Custom CTI Provider").
      • Provider ID: Assign a unique ID to the provider for identification purposes.
      • Provider URL: Input the URL where the widget is hosted (e.g., https://yourserver.com/path-to-widget/index.html).
      • Communication System ID: Specify the ID of the communication system being used.

  3. Validate Add-On Behavior:

    • Test interactions using the widget and confirm that the payloads are processed correctly within the Add-On.

  4. Save the Configuration:

    • Click Save to apply the provider configuration.

  5. Test the Widget:

    • Ensure that the widget loads correctly in the Agent Desktop by testing interactions such as inbound and outbound calls or chats.

Scenario 3: Live Activity in C4C

  1. Host the Widget Files:

    • Ensure the widget files are hosted on a secure server accessible to the C4C system.

  2. Configure for Live Activity:

    • In SAP Customer Cloud, go to Administrator > General Settings > Live Activity Configuration.
    • Provide the URL of the widget in the appropriate field for the Live Activity panel.

  3. Test Live Activity:

    • Initiate a Live Activity interaction and validate that the widget sends payloads correctly.

Conclusion

This POC is a simple but useful tool for SAP Service Cloud developers and testers to validate CTI integrations quickly and efficiently. By retaining all fields and providing flexibility in payload construction, this widget serves as a versatile testing framework for a variety of interaction types.

With the provided configuration steps, you can integrate the widget into multiple SAP platforms, including Service Cloud V2 and C4C, while also extending its functionality to new use cases like Live Activity.

Let us know how you’re using this POC to streamline your integration workflows. If you have questions about the SAP CTI Certification Process, make sure to refer to the SAP KBA 3307629 for additional guidance. 🚀
=================================================================================

Full Code for the POC Widget

Below is the complete code for the POC widget. You can copy and paste it into your own HTML, CSS, and JavaScript files. The widget consists of three main parts: index.html, style.css, and script.js.

1. index.html

 

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>Service Cloud Widget POC</title>
    <meta name="author" content="Lloyd Goveia">
    <meta name="description" content="Service Cloud Widget POC created by Lloyd Goveia. Provided AS IS with no support.">
    <!-- Link to the external CSS file -->
    <link rel="stylesheet" href="style.css">
    <!-- Link to the external JavaScript file -->
    <script defer src="script.js"></script>
</head>
<body>
<!-- Main form for the Service Cloud Widget -->
<form id="callForm" onsubmit="handleSendCall(event)">
    <h1>Service Cloud Widget POC</h1>
    <table>
        <!-- Dropdown to select interaction type -->
        <tr>
            <td><label for="type">Type:</label></td>
            <td>
                <select id="type" name="type" required>
                    <option value="CALL">CALL</option>
                    <option value="CHAT">CHAT</option>
                    <option value="MESSAGE">MESSAGE</option>
                </select>
            </td>
        </tr>
        <!-- Dropdown to select event type -->
        <tr>
            <td><label for="eventType">Event Type:</label></td>
            <td>
                <select id="eventType" name="eventType" required>
                    <option value="INBOUND">INBOUND</option>
                    <option value="OUTBOUND">OUTBOUND</option>
                    <option value="UPDATEACTIVITY">UPDATEACTIVITY</option>
                    <option value="TRANSFER">TRANSFER</option>
                </select>
            </td>
        </tr>
        <!-- Dropdown to select action -->
        <tr>
            <td><label for="action">Action:</label></td>
            <td>
                <select id="action" name="action" required>
                    <option value="NOTIFY">NOTIFY</option>
                    <option value="ACCEPT">ACCEPT</option>
                    <option value="END">END</option>
                </select>
            </td>
        </tr>
        <!-- Phone number field -->
        <tr>
            <td><label for="ani">ANI (Phone Number):</label></td>
            <td><input id="ani" name="ani" type="text" placeholder="+1234567890" value="+14165551234"></td>
        </tr>
        <!-- Email field -->
        <tr>
            <td><label for="email">Email:</label></td>
            <td><input id="email" name="email" type="email" placeholder="example@test.com"></td>
        </tr>
        <!-- Subject field -->
        <tr>
            <td><label for="subject">Subject:</label></td>
            <td><input id="subject" name="subject" type="text" placeholder="Enter subject"></td>
        </tr>
        <!-- Text field -->
        <tr>
            <td><label for="text">Text:</label></td>
            <td><textarea id="text" name="text" placeholder="Enter text"></textarea></td>
        </tr>
        <!-- External Reference ID field -->
        <tr>
            <td><label for="externalReferenceID">External Reference ID:</label></td>
            <td>
                <input id="externalReferenceID" name="externalReferenceID" type="text">
                <button type="button" onclick="generateRandomGUID('externalReferenceID')">Generate Random GUID</button>
            </td>
        </tr>
        <!-- Call recording field -->
        <tr>
            <td><label for="recordingId">Call Recording:</label></td>
            <td><input id="recordingId" name="recordingId" type="text"></td>
        </tr>
        <!-- Submit and Reset buttons -->
        <tr>
            <td colspan="2">
                <button type="submit">Post Payload to Parent Window</button>
                <button type="button" onclick="resetForm()">Reset Form</button>
            </td>
        </tr>
    </table>
    <!-- Section to display the generated XML payload -->
    <div class="payload-message" id="payloadMessage"></div>
</form>
</body>
</html>

 

2. style.css

 

/* Style for the body of the widget */
body {
    font-family: Arial, sans-serif;
    background-color: #f0f0f0;
    margin: 0;
    padding: 20px;
    display: flex;
    justify-content: center;
    align-items: center;
    height: 100vh;
}

/* Style for the form container */
form {
    background-color: #fff;
    padding: 20px;
    border-radius: 8px;
    box-shadow: 0 0 10px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.1);
    width: 100%;
    max-width: 600px;
}

/* Styling for the form header */
h1 {
    text-align: center;
    font-size: 1.5em;
}

/* Styling for input labels */
label {
    font-size: 0.85em;
    font-weight: bold;
    margin-bottom: 5px;
    display: block;
}

/* Styling for input, textarea, and dropdown fields */
input, textarea, select {
    width: calc(100% - 10px);
    padding: 8px;
    border: 1px solid #ccc;
    border-radius: 4px;
}

/* Styling for buttons */
button {
    padding: 6px 15px;
    font-size: 0.85em;
    background-color: #007bff;
    color: #fff;
    border: none;
    border-radius: 4px;
    cursor: pointer;
}

/* Hover effect for buttons */
button:hover {
    background-color: #0056b3;
}

/* Styling for the payload display section */
.payload-message {
    background-color: #e8e8e8;
    color: #333;
    padding: 10px;
    margin-top: 15px;
    border-radius: 4px;
    font-size: 0.9em;
    word-wrap: break-word;
}

 

3. script.js

 

// Define the global c4c object for CTI integration
var c4c = c4c || {};
c4c.cti = c4c.cti || {};
c4c.cti.integration = function () { };

/**
 * Constructs an XML payload from form parameters
 *  {Object} parameters - Key-value pairs to include in the payload
 * @returns {string} - XML payload as a string
 */
c4c.cti.integration.prototype._formXMLPayload = function (parameters) {
    var sPayload = "<?xml version=\"1.0\" encoding=\"utf-8\"?><payload>";
    Object.entries(parameters).forEach(([key, value]) => {
        if (key === "Action" && value === "ACCEPT") {
            value = ""; // Leave Action empty for ACCEPT
        }
        if (value && value.trim() !== "") {
            sPayload += `<${key}>${value}</${key}>`;
        }
    });
    sPayload += "</payload>";
    console.log("Constructed Payload:", sPayload);
    return sPayload;
};

/**
 * Handles form submission and sends the payload to the parent window
 *  {Event} event - Form submission event
 */
function handleSendCall(event) {
    event.preventDefault();
    var parameters = {
        Type: document.getElementById("type").value,
        EventType: document.getElementById("eventType").value,
        Action: document.getElementById("action").value,
        ANI: document.getElementById("ani").value,
        Email: document.getElementById("email").value || "",
        Subject: document.getElementById("subject").value || "",
        Text: document.getElementById("text").value || "",
        ExternalReferenceID: document.getElementById("externalReferenceID").value || "",
        RecordingId: document.getElementById("recordingId").value || ""
    };

    var integration = new c4c.cti.integration();
    integration.sendIncomingCalltoC4C(parameters);
}

/**
 * Generates a random GUID and assigns it to the specified field
 *  {string} fieldId - ID of the field to populate
 */
function generateRandomGUID(fieldId) {
    var guid = crypto.randomUUID().replace(/-/g, '').toUpperCase().substring(0, 35);
    document.getElementById(fieldId).value = guid;
}

/**
 * Resets the form and clears displayed messages
 */
function resetForm() {
    document.getElementById("callForm").reset();
    document.getElementById("payloadMessage").innerText = "";
}

/**
 * Displays the constructed XML payload
 *  {string} payload - XML payload to display
 */
function displayPayloadMessage(payload) {
    var payloadDiv = document.getElementById("payloadMessage");
    payloadDiv.innerText = payload;
    payloadDiv.style.display = "block";
}

 

How to Use This Code

  • Copy the respective code sections into three separate files: index.html, style.css, and script.js.
  • Follow the steps to configure the widget in SAP Service Cloud or SAP Customer Cloud (C4C).
  • Use the POC widget to validate your CTI payloads.

Demo:
https://video.sap.com/media/t/1_zu1o64u0

entry.title: Re: SAP Cloud Integration(CPI): Customizing Email using Mail Adapter
entry.content: Hi All, I am trying to write text over an image. In browser I am able to achieve it however, same code doesn't work in sap cpi mail adapter, like absolute or relative positioning, limitations on CSS like gradient.   2. Add inline annotations, such as text or highlights, on top of the image.

Is there any standard control or feature in SAP MDK to achieve this functionality? If not, are there any compatible NativeScript plugins or workarounds to implement this in SAP MDK?

Any guidance, documentation links, or examples would be greatly appreciated!

Thank you,
Divan Wellalage

entry.title: Re: Duplicate item on binding in SAPUI5 Application
entry.content:
@WG23 

Even if the key value is duplicated, it is displayed normally when calling the CDS Entity from Postman.
but When table binding is performed, data is expressed repeatedly due to the same key value.

This is
It's not a Binding error, it's a CDS key field configuration issue.
You need to change the key field configuration so that the key value is unique.

entry.title: 新功能发布！SAP Analytics Cloud 指南针的通用版本
entry.content:
指南针（Compass）是 SAP Analytics Cloud 即将推出的一个新功能，它旨在帮助用户在面对如通货膨胀、劳动力成本和总收入不在预期范围内等情况时，分析可能的结果。指南针的推出，使得上述问题的解决不再需要多天的讨论，而只需在系统中通过简单点击即可实时获得模拟结果。这个功能预计将在2025年第一季度发布。 

什么是指南针？ 

指南针是一种新的基于蒙特卡洛算法的模拟功能，能够帮助用户在面对含有不确定性的性能驱动因素时分析可能的结果。它能够帮助用户探索公司收入、所需劳动力、销售目标、投资回报率、项目持续时间等多种指标在不同因素变动的影响下的可能结果。指南针提供了一个以最终用户为中心的界面，降低了复杂性，在一定程度上消除了数学理论与业务用户之间的障碍。通过简化的操作界面，即使是没有掌握高级的数学知识或计算机知识的非技术用户，也能轻松进行统计学的随机模拟。无需编码，无需数据或公式的复制；只需要单击并运行！指南针将包括以下功能： 

场景建模与比较 

用户可以为多个驱动因素的波动创建“what-if”模拟场景，并使用场景将它们分组为不同的分类。这对于比较策略或不可控事件的影响尤其有用。如果需要，用户可以选择显示选定场景的结果，以便比较每个场景涉及的可能性和风险。 

多维模拟 

用户可以配置不同维度和组合（例如，产品、地区、公司代码、业务单位、期间等）的可能的业绩波动，并模拟它们对相关目标的复合影响。系统所提供的灵活界面还允许在不进行任何编码的情况下更改多个维度的驱动因素。例如：系统允许将地理/业务细分方面快速集成到风险分析中，并同时对所作假设的过程保持透明。 

对悲观/现实/乐观情况的洞察 

每个场景的影响分布和风险环境都可以通过悲观、现实和乐观情况的图形表示，从而变得易于理解。每种情况都分别包含了边界值和以百分位形式表示的概率。用户可对每种情况的颜色、名称或百分位数等元素进行自定义以添加更多的上下文。 

针对特定目标的风险环境研究 

通过使用指南针，用户可以选择分析驱动因素不确定性对目标的整体影响分布，或者深入聚焦于分析实现特定目标的可能性。例如，用户可以轻松回答这样的问题：“我们今年赚五千万美元的机会有多大？”结合对悲观、现实和乐观情况的洞察，用户可以快速理解目标是否倾向于激进或保守，从而获得可以与主观解释相对照的，涉及相关风险的客观定义。 

私有场景和公共场景的协作 

在指南针模拟功能中，用户所创建的场景可以用于个人探索或者公开披露给共享用户。这使得模拟协作和假设的可追溯性成为可能，如果需要，甚至可以向下追溯到个人层面。此外，共享权限由已建立的文件存储库框架管理，并具有与共享模型、故事或任何其他 SAP Analytics Cloud 构件相似的灵活性和可控性。 

模拟持久性 

用户可以将模拟与模型和其他部件一起保存在文件资源库中，以便管理和重用。这样做不仅可以促进协作，还可实现概念可追溯：过去所做的假设可以轻松被检索，用户可将检索到的假设与实际情况进行比较，以此来验证模拟方法是否有效。长期这样做，有助于用户更好地了解自己公司内部的动态数据。 

驱动因素不确定性配置 

在选定的KPI模型中，所有可能的驱动因素将被自动检测，并且可以立即开始模拟。只需用户输入所需驱动因素的预期最小值和最大值，就可以将这些值作为指导来影响计算。还可以选择正态分布或均匀分布类型来建模波动行为。 

一键激活/停用不确定性影响 

用户只需单击一下，就可以在模拟中选择包含或排除不确定性影响，无需重新建模场景。这使得在进行假设的概念分组时，用户能够灵活地进行现场实验。 

许可证 

指南针是 SAP Analytics Cloud 的内置功能，将随计划标准许可一起提供。 

加入我们的旅程 

随着指南针（Compass）的正式发布，我们迈出了实现您可能在 Data Unleashed 2024中已经看到的长期愿景的第一步。虽然这个初始通用版本不会集成人工智能（AI），但我们的愿景依然清晰，致力于不断改进指南针。我们邀请您加入我们的旅程，通过试用指南针来进一步推进其发展。我们期待您的反馈，以帮助我们更好地了解 influence portal 如何为您提供帮助。 

请使用产品路线图来关注指南针的最新动态。

entry.title: Managing replication flow Delta load dependency on next subsequent step run
entry.content:
Hi, Currently we are facing load dependency related issues while using delta Transformation flow, Is there a solution or workaround available for the below scenario?

Currently, we can only schedule replication flows based on time intervals (minutes or hours), not at specific times. Also due to this interval next run gets delay by few Minute and within a month it may reach to an hour.
Is it possible to schedule replication flows to run at specific times if not is there a way to have the dependency between the delta replication flow and the other target flow(transformation flow & Data flow) to ensure that the target flow runs only once the replication flow is completed?

entry.title: How Generative AI is Transforming Supply Chain Planning with SAP Solutions
entry.content:
Understanding Generative AI

Before we dive into the specifics of SAP, let’s talk about what generative AI actually is. Unlike traditional AI, which analyzes existing data to make predictions, generative AI creates new content. This could mean generating supply chain models, optimizing routes, or even proposing innovative solutions for logistical challenges.

Why is this important for supply chains? Because supply chains thrive on adaptability, and generative AI offers the tools to build new strategies when the unexpected happens. Think of it as a Swiss Army knife—always ready with a solution for any scenario.

The Role of AI in Modern Supply Chains

AI is no longer just a buzzword. It’s an operational necessity in today’s fast-paced world. From real-time demand forecasting to streamlining manufacturing, AI has transformed supply chains into intelligent ecosystems. But the real magic happens when generative AI steps in. It’s not just about analyzing data; it’s about creating solutions that didn’t exist before.

SAP Solutions Enhancing Supply Chain Efficiency

SAP, a leader in enterprise software, has gone all-in on AI to tackle supply chain inefficiencies. Their tools don’t just stop at automating tasks; they drive real insight and innovation. Drawing from our experience, SAP’s solutions work seamlessly to connect data points across an organization, ensuring end-to-end visibility.

Key Features of SAP's AI-Driven Supply Chain Solutions

  1. Integrated Data Platforms: SAP integrates data from multiple sources to provide a single, unified view.
  2. Predictive Analytics: Helps in anticipating demand, reducing waste, and planning better.
  3. Automation at Scale: Streamlines repetitive tasks, allowing teams to focus on strategic initiatives.

Generative AI Capabilities in SAP

SAP has added a layer of generative AI to its offerings, and the impact is remarkable. Our team discovered through using this product that it helps businesses:

  • Generate real-time supply chain models that respond to disruptions.
  • Create alternative scenarios for demand fluctuations.
  • Optimize supplier relationships by suggesting innovative contract terms.

Overview of Generative AI Applications

Generative AI isn’t just about clever algorithms; it’s about practical, impactful applications. For instance:

  • Dynamic Route Planning: Generative AI can map out the fastest and most cost-effective transportation routes.
  • Inventory Optimization: Avoid stockouts and overstock situations by generating precise inventory strategies.

Benefits for Supply Chain Planning

  • Faster Decision-Making: Real-time data insights enable instant responses.
  • Improved Forecast Accuracy: Advanced predictive models minimize risks.
  • Operational Efficiency: Save costs and reduce manual intervention.

Transforming Decision-Making Processes

Real-Time Data Utilization

With generative AI, supply chain managers don’t just rely on historical data. Instead, real-time data becomes the cornerstone of decision-making. For example, SAP’s AI solutions analyze live market conditions to adjust production schedules on the fly.

Predictive Analytics and Forecasting

After putting it to the test, we determined through our experiments that SAP’s generative AI delivers unparalleled predictive accuracy. This allows businesses to anticipate disruptions like raw material shortages or shipping delays and plan accordingly.

Challenges and Considerations in Implementation

While generative AI offers exciting possibilities, it’s not without challenges. Based on our firsthand experience, businesses must address the following:

Data Privacy and Ethical Use of AI

Ensuring compliance with data privacy regulations is paramount. Generative AI relies on large datasets, and improper handling could lead to ethical concerns.

Integrating AI with Existing Systems

Legacy systems often present integration hurdles. However, SAP’s modular architecture makes it easier to adopt generative AI without overhauling your entire tech stack.

Case Study: Abto Software's Approach to AI in Supply Chains

Overview of Abto Software's Solutions

Abto Software, a prominent .NET development company, has been at the forefront of AI integration in supply chains. They specialize in creating custom AI solutions that complement SAP’s capabilities, focusing on predictive maintenance and real-time analytics.

Impact on Supply Chain Efficiency

Through our practical knowledge, we’ve seen Abto’s tools help businesses:

  • Reduce downtime by 30%.
  • Improve demand forecasting accuracy by 25%.
  • Cut transportation costs by optimizing delivery routes.

Future Trends in Supply Chain Management with AI

The Shift Towards Autonomous Supply Chains

Imagine a supply chain that runs itself—where decisions are made autonomously. This is no longer a distant dream, thanks to generative AI. As per our expertise, the next wave of innovation will focus on achieving full autonomy.

Emerging Technologies Supporting AI Integration

Technologies like IoT (Internet of Things) and blockchain are pivotal for AI integration. For example, IoT devices provide real-time data, while blockchain ensures secure and transparent data sharing.

Comparative Analysis of SAP's AI Solutions

Feature

Traditional Methods

SAP AI Solutions

Decision-Making Speed

Slow

Real-time insights

Data Processing Capability

Limited

Advanced analytics

Predictive Accuracy

Moderate

High with machine learning

Conclusion: The Future of Supply Chain Planning with Generative AI

Generative AI is more than just a buzzword; it’s the future of supply chain planning. By enabling real-time decision-making, improving accuracy, and fostering innovation, tools like SAP’s AI solutions are transforming the supply chain landscape. Whether it’s reducing costs or enhancing efficiency, the benefits are clear. For businesses ready to embrace this technology, the potential is limitless.

FAQs

  1. What is generative AI in supply chain planning? Generative AI creates new strategies and models to optimize supply chains, going beyond traditional predictive analytics.
  2. How does SAP leverage generative AI? SAP uses generative AI for real-time data analysis, predictive forecasting, and dynamic decision-making.
  3. What are the main benefits of generative AI in supply chains? It improves decision-making speed, enhances predictive accuracy, and reduces operational costs.
  4. What challenges come with implementing AI in supply chains? Key challenges include data privacy concerns, system integration, and the need for large datasets.
  5. How does Abto Software support AI in supply chains? Abto Software provides custom solutions for predictive maintenance, real-time analytics, and more.
  6. What’s the future of AI in supply chain management? The future lies in autonomous supply chains powered by generative AI and supported by IoT and blockchain.
  7. Why should businesses consider SAP for supply chain AI? SAP offers robust, scalable, and innovative AI solutions that integrate seamlessly with existing systems.

entry.title: The Power of Personal and Corporate Action in Achieving Sustainability
entry.content:
My eyes jolted when I was reading through a featured article on the UNDP (United Nations Development Programme) website the other day about the desertification of our lands. The article, 'Nature, Climate and Land', points out that we are losing an area equivalent to four football fields every second to desertification. That’s alarming. Some of us, as city dwellers, are so busy in our daily grind that we are clueless about the massive degradation happening around us. It’s no wonder that the 2030 Sustainable Development Goals of the United Nations are off track. Of late, I have been questioning how much we really know about sustainability and what we’re doing about it.

Sustainability is a word we hear everywhere, but what does it really mean?

Simply put, it’s about using the earth’s resources wisely so that future generations can also have what they need. It’s about creating balance in our lives and living in a way that doesn’t harm nature.

When we talk about sustainability, we often think it’s up to big companies and governments to make the change. But that’s not true. Lately, I have been encouraging my colleagues and friends that each of us has a role to play. We can’t just depend on corporations to save the planet. The power to make a difference starts with us and it starts most probably from our own backyard !

Take trees, for example. We know they give us oxygen and clean the air. But they do much more: They help cool the earth, protect soil, and give shelter to many animals. If we want to protect our planet, saving trees is one of the simplest and most important things we can do. But it’s not just about trees. It's about taking care of nature in every way we can.

Every small change we make matters. It could be planting a tree in your backyard, reducing plastic use, or supporting local, sustainable products. These might seem like small things, but if millions of us do them, the impact can be huge.

The United Nations has set 17 Sustainable Development Goals, and protecting nature is a key part of achieving them. And this is something we can all do. Given the urgent situation, the United Nations has organized a COP (Conference of the Parties) on Desertification and Land Degradation this December. As I write this article, the conference is currently underway, I think.  Our individual actions can make a significant impact  when we make sustainability and sustainable living as our goal. Because when we all do our part, we can create a better, more sustainable future.

entry.title: The ABAP Detective Charges by the Hour, Plus Expenses
entry.content:
Prior to current smart meter tech that can broadcast time series data to remote sensor collections, when an electric meter was "read" it was done someone physically viewing the device. In my youth, a gas meter inside the house required a meter reader to enter the premises. The bill was prepared monthly so the most analytical comparison was month-to-month, or "this month a year ago". Now, with hourly metrics stored and forwarded, analysis is much more granular.

The detecting part of the tale is how to go from the provided data to a database, or in this case, multiple repositories. Let's look at the available content, or usage records.

 

TYPE,DATE,START TIME,END TIME,USAGE (kWh),COST,NOTES
Electric usage,2023-12-01,00:00,00:59,0.54,$0.08
Electric usage,2023-12-01,01:00,01:59,0.61,$0.09
Electric usage,2023-12-01,02:00,02:59,1.01,$0.15
Electric usage,2023-12-01,03:00,03:59,3.51,$0.54
Electric usage,2023-12-01,04:00,04:59,3.19,$0.49
Electric usage,2023-12-01,05:00,05:59,5.80,$0.89
Electric usage,2023-12-01,06:00,06:59,2.93,$0.45
Electric usage,2023-12-01,07:00,07:59,4.61,$0.89
Electric usage,2023-12-01,08:00,08:59,2.00,$0.39
Electric usage,2023-12-01,09:00,09:59,1.04,$0.20
Electric usage,2023-12-01,10:00,10:59,1.13,$0.22

 

Nicely formatted, comma separated, and apparently complete records (no obvious missing columns or rows). There is a start time and an end time in minutes, so the first question is which to use? I picked the latter with the idea of consumption rather than current (pun intended). My initial transformation code used Perl to reformat these columns into a style usable to load into Zabbix via that open-source monitoring system's "trapper" function.

A snip of the 1st version:

 

  print "container ", ;
  print "ElectricUsage ", ;
  print str2time($f2 . " " . $f4);

 

The slick str2time function from Date::Parse emits the transformed input time/date into an epoch value (seconds past Jan 1, 1970) with no need to define particular field content. The output then looks like:

 

container ElectricUsage 1730422740 0.60
container ElectricUsage 1730426340 0.60
container ElectricUsage 1730429940 0.98
container ElectricUsage 1730433540 0.60
container ElectricUsage 1730437140 0.60
container ElectricUsage 1730440740 0.60
container ElectricUsage 1730444340 0.59
container ElectricUsage 1730447940 0.63
container ElectricUsage 1730451540 0.63
container ElectricUsage 1730455140 0.63

 

I can plug  these watts into a command line data loader:

zabbix_sender -vv -z ${host} -p 10051 -T -i $datafile

So far, so good? Yes, except we didn't check the results. The non-obvious error is which time zone pertains to the source information, and does the loaded data appear at the correct (local) time? The source data typically is in the local timezone (Eastern US in this case), but the database server runs on UTC. The clue was time-shifted charts that mangled the correct view. This was the tale from 2 years back: the abap detective gets their clock cleaned, The quick fix I used to set the timezone was an environment variable (in UNIX) prior to loading data:

export TZ=EST5EDT

This worked as long as the source data was truly in that zone. To be sure of this, I looked again at the data feeds and came up with this quality control check on the epoch value since it appears to be a random number:

date -r 1704085140
Mon Jan 1 04:59:00 UTC 2024

(this date command flag is built-in to BSD UNIX; other flavors need different flags/formats).

If this data was supposed to be from midnight to 1:00, the timestamp is skewed. Back to read the manual, again. Ah, the date parse routine has an optional second parameter defining the timezone (though it looks like that could be omitted if the time string included a zone).

str2time(DATE [, ZONE])

Maybe the function code is right, maybe not, we should do a line-up and check.

 

#!/usr/pkg/bin/perl
use Date::Parse;
print "no zone: ", str2time("2024-11-01" . " " . "00:59"),             "\n";
print "EST    : ", str2time("2024-11-01" . " " . "00:59", "EST"),      "\n";
print "EST5EDT: ", str2time("2024-11-01" . " " . "00:59", "EST5EDT"),  "\n";
print "UTC    : ", str2time("2024-11-01" . " " . "00:59", "UTC"),      "\n";

 

Ah, not quite the answer I expected:

 

$ perl  str2time_check.pl
no zone: 1730422740
EST    : 1730440740
EST5EDT: 1730422740
UTC    : 1730422740

 

Another functional approach:

$ TZ=EST date -r 1730440740
Fri Nov  1 00:59:00 EST 2024

$ TZ=EST5EDT date -r 1730440740
Fri Nov  1 01:59:00 EDT 2024

I omitted testing for other timezones that might be used, such as "EDT" (eastern daylight time), and "Americas/New York" or "Americas/Detroit" that other time functions might respect as "close enough" once I saw which gave the expected answer and which did not.

Daylight savings time, or other periodic clock changes, can make simple tasks complex. When we used the AutoSys enterprise scheduling suite, you could define periodic jobs (including SAP batch runs) on a wide variety of intervals. Jobs defined to run during time changes (clock jumps to or fro 1 hour) were managed internally logic that would not run any twice when repeating an hour, and tried to squeeze in any workload that would have been missed when skipping ahead by running them accelerated, in order, after the skip. For electronic record retention, what does that look like?

Skip ahead:

Electric usage,2024-03-10,00:00,00:59,0.55,$0.09
Electric usage,2024-03-10,01:00,01:59,0.54,$0.09,* This data was estimated
Electric usage,2024-03-10,03:00,03:59,0.57,$0.09
Electric usage,2024-03-10,04:00,04:59,1.26,$0.20
Electric usage,2024-03-10,05:00,05:59,3.62,$0.57
Electric usage,2024-03-10,06:00,06:59,2.29,$0.36

Jump back:

Electric usage,2024-11-02,23:00,23:59,1.04,$0.16
Electric usage,2024-11-03,00:00,00:59,0.52,$0.08
Electric usage,2024-11-03,01:00,01:59,0.50,$0.08
Electric usage,2024-11-03,02:00,02:59,0.51,$0.08
Electric usage,2024-11-03,03:00,03:59,0.49,$0.08
Electric usage,2024-11-03,04:00,04:59,0.47,$0.07
Electric usage,2024-11-03,05:00,05:59,0.50,$0.08

I expected to see, maybe, double metrics for the hour that repeats in November, yet I don't. Another case for another day or time.

In the past, monthly charts were all the rage; now it's "peak usage" hourly/work week graphs.

Monthly data collections and comparisons

Legacy data capture by month:

01/01/19971880
12/01/19961623
11/01/1996839
10/01/1996623
09/01/1996821
08/01/1996766
07/01/1996955
06/01/1996729
05/01/1996948
04/01/19961590
03/01/19961726
02/01/19962492
01/01/19962116

Graphs of hourly metrics (and yes, the clock-driven heating system starts up at 0700).

Hourly chart for a day of electrons

This source chart might be useful to verify locally transformed data visualizations as another quality check.

Another view showing data over a one week period:

Hourly data over 1 week

Also a week, this time specified by the handy strings "now-14d" and "now-7d":

More detail for a week, adding an ambient temperature value (this built-in feature often does not render well with Zabbix if the units and values differ wildly):

Spikes in energy usage correspond to ambient temperature drops. Besides the data quality control aspect, these trends might hint at ways to reduce energy by showing the times of high usage. A better correlation would require custom data retrieval.

Lastly, ambient temperature inside the dwelling (above chart is from the National Weather Service at a nearby airport). 

Yes, this room is warmer than ambient, partly from heat pump, and partly from sunlight.

From a pure database perspective, I also pushed the energy values into a PostgreSQL database not related to Zabbix in a different method than the above. I could load the CSV values into a cache table, then create materialized views bucketing by month or hour.

db=> \d  bge_electric_hourly_usage;
                    Table "db.electric_hourly_usage"
      Column      |            Type             | Collation | Nullable | Default
------------------+-----------------------------+-----------+----------+---------
 usage_date_start | timestamp without time zone |           | not null |
 usage_date_end   | timestamp without time zone |           | not null |
 usage            | double precision            |           | not null |
 cost             | double precision            |           |          |
 notes            | character(255)              |           |          |
create materialized view db.electric_daily_usage (
  usage_date    ,
  usage         ,
  cost 
)
as
select
  min(usage_date_start) as usage_date,  -- timestamp without timezone
  sum(usage)            as usage,       -- double precision
  sum(cost)             as cost         -- double precision
from
  db.electric_hourly_usage
group by
  extract(YEAR from   usage_date_start),
  extract(MONTH from  usage_date_start),
  extract(DAY from    usage_date_start)
order BY
  extract(YEAR from   usage_date_start),
  extract(MONTH from  usage_date_start),
  extract(DAY from    usage_date_start)
;

A future enhancement will be to reflect the timezone values in the data dictionary, since the current (no pun intended) columns have none. I also see I used start of the hour instead of end.

References:

  • Zabbix - zabbix.com/documentation/current/en/manual/config/items/itemtypes/trapper 
  • Green Button (energy use) - www.energy.gov/data/green-button 
  • Perl "time local" - metacpan.org/pod/Time::Local 
  • A different date/epoch function (from Broadcom/CA/AutoSys): time0() time command calculate internal ca workload automation ae time 
This example converts the AutoSys Workload Automation 
date and time to MM/DD/YYYY hh:mm:ss format:

time0 -A 1121265931

The return from the command resembles the following:

CAUAJM I 50097 External Time: 07/13/2005 07:45:31

entry.title: SAP AI SDKs and where to find them (Follow up blog)
entry.content:
Follow up on blog post https://community.sap.com/t5/artificial-intelligence-and-machine-learning-blogs/fantastic-sap-ai-sdks-and-where-to-find-them/ba-p/13610510

December 2024 SDK updates

New Java, JavaScript and TypeScript SDK Releases!

1. SAP Cloud SDK (5.0) for AI was released with JavaScript/TypeScript and support for orchestration services grounding capabilities. Generative AI Hub SDK version 4.1.1 introduced support for prompt registry templates in orchestration. Check out https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/introducing-the-sap-cloud-sdk-for-ai-javascript-typescript/ba-p/13892856

  • AI Orchestration with @sap-ai-sdk/orchestration: This package facilitates the use of the Generative AI Hub's orchestration service, offering functionalities such as templating, content filtering, data masking, and grounding. These tools help customize AI interactions, content safety, and integrate external data sources.

  • AI Management with @sap-ai-sdk/ai-api: This package automates processes like creating artifacts, configurations, and deployments, executing batch inference jobs, and managing Docker registries and object storage for training data. It provides tools for managing scenarios and workflows in SAP AI Core.

  • LangChain Integration with @sap-ai-sdk/langchain: This package offers LangChain-compatible clients for Azure OpenAI models deployed in SAP AI Core. It supports integration, flexible client initialization, and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) workflows for more advanced use cases.

  • Generative AI with @sap-ai-sdk/foundation-models: This package provides streamlined access to Azure OpenAI specific generative AI models available via the Generative AI Hub, focusing on direct model interactions for inference and embedding requests without additional layers like templating or content filtering.

by Author

 

2. Version 1.0 of the SAP Cloud SDK for AI for Java was released, it provides tools for workflow and scenario management within SAP AI Core. Java, though complicated, its still one of the kings of Enterprise solutions, a rich ecosystem of libraries and frameworks, like Spring, Hibernate, and Jakarta EE. Spring is quite popular for developing microservices-based architectures like BTP and has multiple Github repos.

Check out blog https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/introducing-the-sap-cloud-sdk-for-ai-java/ba-p/13956162

Upcoming SDK Releases (future Blog updates)

1. ABAP SDK planned for Q1 2025
2. Enhanced ABAP developer capabilities with an LLM trained specifically on ABAP code for 2025

Original Blog

https://community.sap.com/t5/artificial-intelligence-and-machine-learning-blogs/fantastic-sap-ai-sdks-and-where-to-find-them/ba-p/13610510

entry.title: Re: How to Call RAP Actions with Table Types from a List Report
entry.content:
Hi @sameera1213,

Action URL can be constructed as below:

  • static action : /<EntityName>/<NameSpace>.<ActionName>
  • instance action: /<EntityName>(key1=<value>,key2=<value>)/<NameSpace>.<ActionName>

You can find the OData namespace in the metadata file.

entry.title: Reflecting on 2024: Unveiling AI Breakthroughs That Redefined Innovation
entry.content:
As 2024 comes to a close, it's time to reflect on the remarkable advancements in the world of AI that have defined this year. Based on my experiences, practices, and observations, I've identified five standout developments that have left a lasting impression. These are not just technological marvels but also pivotal milestones in the journey of AI's evolution. For some of these, I've already shared detailed insights in my blogs, which I'll link for those who wish to explore further.

  1. The Emergence of Reasoning Models: This September, we witnessed the introduction of reasoning models that fundamentally redefined how AI models are trained. These models employed a novel training methodology called TTC, which set the stage for future model development. Unlike traditional LLMs, which faced constraints in terms of data and compute power, reasoning models pave the way for using synthetic data and reduced computational requirements. This innovation addresses the energy concerns associated with current AI technologies and promises a more sustainable future for model development. For a detailed exploration of this topic, you can check out my blog: AI's Growth Paradox: Why More Data Isn't Always Better.
  2. AI-Based Search Revolution: This year marked a pivotal shift in how we interact with search engines, with AI-based search emerging as a game-changer. Perplexity has taken the lead in this market, challenging the dominance of Google, which now finds itself playing catch-up with its AI overview offerings. The competition has sparked innovations, and we anticipate a Perplexity-style solution from Google soon. On a personal note, my favourite development in this space is OpenAI's Search functionality, which was made available to everyone this December. For an in-depth look at this trend, please read my blog: From Blue Links to Direct Answers: My Journey with AI Search.
  3. Advancements in AI Video Applications: This year's progress in AI-powered video applications has been remarkable. OpenAI's launch of Sora has set new benchmarks, while Google's VEO2 is gaining traction with positive reviews. Tools like Heygen have also shown encouraging advancements, signalling a promising future for video AI technologies. As we move into 2025, the potential for more groundbreaking developments in this area is immense. You can check out some of my experiments with Sora here: Sora AI - Experiments and Thoughts.
  4. The Rise of AI Agents: Agents are set to become a defining trend as we head into 2025. This year saw notable launches by enterprises like Salesforce and SAP, showcasing the potential of AI agents in transforming business operations. Among startups, ElevenLabs stood out with its impressive voice AI module, which has caught my attention as a groundbreaking innovation. Both horizontal and vertical agents will play significant roles in this space. Vertical agents will be industry-specific or LOB-specific, such as customer service agents designed to address sector-specific challenges in healthcare or retail. Horizontal agents, on the other hand, will operate across industries and LOBs, like AI-driven workflow optimization tools or virtual assistants that support general enterprise functions.ty. I wouldn't be surprised if major enterprises show interest in acquiring or collaborating with such startups shortly. For a deeper understanding of the transformative power of AI agents, read my explainer blog: The Rise of AI Agents: Transforming Business and Beyond.
  5. NotebookLM and the AI-Powered Podcast Revolution: Among the most impressive AI applications this year was NotebookLM. This tool converts documents into podcasts grounded in factual data with minimal hallucinations. This innovation is a boon for students and researchers, streamlining access to information in an audio-friendly format. ElevenLabs has also developed similar functionality, reinforcing the growing potential in this domain. I've personally experimented with NotebookLM, and the results were remarkable. You can check out my experiment here: Agentic AI by Subhankar Pattanayak.

Reflecting on 2024, it is clear that the year was all about experimentation. Looking ahead to 2025, we anticipate enterprises formalizing these experiments to derive real value. Companies that lead in AI adoption will undoubtedly deliver more to their customers, while internal AI integration within enterprises will continue to grow. Microsoft's Copilot has already set a high standard, and innovations like Anthropic's Artefacts and OpenAI's Canvas suggest that innovation will only accelerate.

Here's wishing you and your family happy holidays and a fantastic 2025. May the coming year bring more breakthroughs, lessons, and opportunities to embrace AI's transformative power!

entry.title: Invalid ## characters showing in user menu in SAP S4HANA
entry.content:
Symptom : 

We have installed the ZH - Simplified Chinese Language in S4HANA 2022 FPS 01 system and when we login with ZH Language in sap system, We could see some ### characters in user menu.

Resolution :

1. Implement the SAP Note #3413131 in your S4HANA system along with its post- manual activity. This Note is valid for S4HANA 2020 to S4HANA 2023. If your system is lower than mentioned release, Please follow the steps 1 and 2 of resolution procedure in SAP Note #2068736.

2. After implementation of this note, run the newly added report RPRGN_UPD_ROLE_MENU_TXT. This report can also be accessed via SEC_SUPPORT under role administration.

  • Report RPRGN_UPD_ROLE_MENU_TXT comes fully ready for execution.
  • The only inputs that need consideration is the 'User Name' and the option 'Type of Change' which is self explanatory, 
  • If your user is currently facing an issue where their Easy Access Menu is filled with ##### you simply need to select 'Replace Menu Texts with #'.
  • You can even run the report in Test Mode as it is an option.
  • The language you wish to add to the menu, which in my case is ZH, has to be the logon language to properly run this report since under 'Languages' the 'Target Language' is not changeable.

Reference:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0002068736

https://me.sap.com/notes/3413131

Hope this blog will help you to solve your issue too.

Requesting all readers to drop your feedback or thoughts in comments, also post your questions, if you have any, I will try to answer to the best of my knowledge and research.

Do follow my profile to see more useful contents related to SAP BASIS and HANA topics in near future.

entry.title: Supplier Disqualification through Supplier data API with Pagination for Process Project
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

We have configured or automated the supplier qualification through Process Project using the Supplier Data API with Pagination. We now have a requirement where we want to discontinue or disqualify the suppliers based on the certificate expiration for specific region and commodity combination. (The certificates from suppliers are obtained through modular questionnaires). Can someone confirm if we have a situation where we have a supplier qualified for more than one region and there is a process project created for that (One Process Project with multiple regions or multiple process projects by each region), can we use the Supplier Data API with pagination to discontinue or disqualify the supplier for one specific region and commodity combination alone? If so, can it be done with the same process type used for creating the qualification or should we be using a different process type for disqualification? If we use a different process type for disqualification, it is creating a new process project and updating the supplier as disqualified but it's not allowing us to requalify the supplier even manually once the status is disqualified. Is this a limitation or anyone is able to achieve the supplier qualification and disqualification through API for Process Projects?

NOTE: We went through the standard SAP Documents and understood that the traditional supplier disqualification is not supported with Process Projects. The disqualification we are looking is on the process project only.

Thanks,
Velu

entry.title: Re: SAP Cloud Platform Integration - Invalid backend resp...
entry.content:
I had the exact same error coming from the SCC from a different scenario. A change in the SCC settings worked for me. I guess it would work in many different situations if you receive this specific error.

I know it's an old topic, but if you encounter this error; Configure the SAP Cloud Connector so that it can process HTTP response headers that are larger than the default 8 kB:

In the 'Cloud Connector Administration' , go to Connector-> Configuration -> Advanced -> Max. HTTP Response Header Length (kb) and set this to the recommended value (30).

My source: SAP-note 2875647 - "SAPUI5 ABAP Repository Load via OData: URL parameters to reduce size of messages in HTTP response header".

entry.title: HDS - Historical Data Service
entry.content:
I have been a technical consultant at SAP Concur for two and a half years and have worked with some customers who needed access to historical data after a merger or acquisition and discontinued the use of past databases.

In this post, I will explain how HDS can help our customers to access to the historical data needed to support an internal audit, inquiry or reporting activities.

The Historical Data Service (HDS) is designed for SAP Concur Professional edition customers who are terminating all existing SAP Concur services, including Travel, and wish to maintain access to historical data for audit, compliance, and reporting purposes. This service is particularly beneficial for organizations undergoing mergers, acquisitions, or restructuring, where consolidation of data is necessary.

It allows designated users to view and print essential documents such as receipts, expense reports, invoices, and travel reports, ensuring that businesses can meet their audit and compliance needs even after terminating their Concur services.

Features:

- Restricted access to historical data for up to five approved administrators with Processor Audit and Reporting roles.

- Ability to view and print images, expense reports, invoices, purchase requests, purchase orders, travel reports, and more.

- Ongoing access to the Cognos reporting environment, including custom reports.

- Deactivation of all other users to ensure data security.

 

Required Services and Components (Landscape):

- SAP Concur Professional edition.

- Termination of all existing SAP Concur services, including Travel.

- Cognos reporting environment for continued access to custom reports.

 

Implementation Considerations:

- Designate a key contact within the organization to manage the HDS project.

- Identify and confirm five users who will have read-only access to historical data.

- Ensure all other users are deactivated to maintain data security.

 

Exclusions:

- HDS is not available for Standard edition customers.

- Not suitable for customers planning to keep their travel services active.

- Not applicable for customers who are not fully terminating all existing SAP Concur services.

More info: HDS

entry.title: Import external service odata v4 , Error : No metadata factory provided
entry.content:
After generating my CAP project, I want to import and use external service odata (OP or Private Cloud). If I download an edmx odata v2 file from sap business hub api or from the AS, the command “cds import myedmx.edmx” works correctly.

However, if I take an edmx file from an odata v4 service from https://api.sap.com/api/OP_PRODUCT_0002/overview or https://api.sap.com/api/OP_PURCHASEORDER_0001/overview

or directly from AS

I got this error:

cds import ..\OP_PRODUCT_0002.edmx                 
Error: No metadata factory provided. Can not resolve 'com.sap.vocabularies.Common.v1'. Please add one via Strategy.setMetadataFactory(function(type, callback){})
    at C:\Users\\AppData\Roaming\npm\node_modules\@sap\cds-dk\lib\import\odata\metadataconverter\MetadataConverter.js:1095:22
    at Context._getEdm (C:\Users\\AppData\Roaming\npm\node_modules\@sap\cds-dk\lib\import\odata\metadataconverter\MetadataConverter.js:936:51)
    at C:\Users\\AppData\Roaming\npm\node_modules\@sap\cds-dk\lib\import\odata\metadataconverter\MetadataConverter.js:986:25
    at new Promise (<anonymous>)

entry.title: Step By Step Guide to Transport Table Contents from One Environment to Other in SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
Transporting table contents in SAP S/4HANA should be approached with caution, especially for large tables or tables containing transactional data. Direct table content transports are generally discouraged for production systems due to potential data inconsistencies. However, for smaller tables, configuration tables, or development/testing purposes, there are methods. Here's a step-by-step guide focusing on the recommended approaches and highlighting the risks:

Method 1: Using Transport of Copies (Recommended for Configuration and Small Tables):

This is the most common and generally recommended approach for transporting table contents. It's suitable for configuration tables or small amounts of master data.

  1. Create a Transport Request: In the source system (development or quality), use transaction SE01 or SE10 to create a new transport request.

  2. Add Table Contents to the Transport Request:

    • Use transaction SM30 (Maintain Table) or SE16N (Data Browser).
    • Enter the table name.
    • Select the entries you want to transport (or all entries if needed, but be cautious with large tables).
    • In SM30, you can use the "Transport" function (usually under "Table" -> "Transport"). In SE16N, use the "Table Content Transport" function (usually under "Utilities" -> "Table Content Transport").
    • Assign the transport request you created in step 1.

  3. Release the Transport Request: In the source system, use transaction SE01 or SE10 to release the transport request. This exports the table contents to transport files.

  4. Import the Transport Request: In the target system (quality or production, with extreme caution for production), use transaction STMS (Transport Management System) to import the transport request.

Method 2: Using SAP Data Services (For Large Data Volumes and Complex Scenarios):

For large tables or complex data transformations, SAP Data Services is the recommended solution. It offers robust data extraction, transformation, and loading (ETL) capabilities.

  1. Design a Data Services Job: Create a Data Services job that extracts data from the source table.
  2. Transform the Data (If Necessary): Apply any required data transformations or cleansing rules.
  3. Load the Data into the Target Table: Configure the job to load the data into the corresponding table in the target system.
  4. Execute the Data Services Job: Run the job to transfer the data.

Method 3: Using ABAP Programs (For Specific Requirements and Complex Logic):

For highly specific requirements or complex logic, you can develop an ABAP program to extract and insert data.

  1. Develop an ABAP Program: Create an ABAP program that reads data from the source table using SELECT statements and inserts it into the target table using INSERT statements.
  2. Create a Transport Request: Add the ABAP program to a transport request.
  3. Transport and Execute the Program: Transport the request to the target system and execute the program.

Important Considerations and Cautions:

  • Consistency: Transporting table contents directly can lead to data inconsistencies if related data in other tables is not transported. Ensure data consistency before and after the transport.
  • Dependencies: Be aware of dependencies between tables. If you transport data from one table, you might need to transport data from related tables as well.
  • Large Tables: Avoid transporting large tables directly, especially in production systems. This can cause performance issues and downtime. Use SAP Data Services or other ETL tools for large data volumes.
  • Transactional Data: Do not transport transactional data (e.g., sales orders, purchase orders) using these methods. Use standard SAP processes like ALE/IDoc or other integration technologies.
  • Production Systems: Exercise extreme caution when transporting table contents to production systems. Thoroughly test the transport in a non-production environment first. Always have a backup of the target system before importing any table content transports.
  • Use Standard SAP Functionality Whenever Possible: Before resorting to direct table transports, explore standard SAP functionalities like customizing transports, master data transports, or application-specific data transfer tools.

Example (Method 1 - SM30):

Let's say you want to transport entries from table T001W (Plants):

  1. SE01 - Create a transport request.
  2. SM30 - Enter T001W.
  3. Click "Maintain."
  4. Select the plant entries you want to transport.
  5. Go to "Table" -> "Transport."
  6. Assign your transport request.
  7. SE01 - Release the transport request.
  8. STMS - Import the transport request into the target system.

By following these steps and considering the important cautions, you can transport table contents safely and effectively in SAP S/4HANA for appropriate use cases. Remember to prioritize data consistency and avoid direct table transports in production for transactional data or large volumes.

entry.title: General tables useful in the SAP S4HANA module MM
entry.content:
The Material Management (MM) module in SAP S/4HANA uses a simplified data model compared to older versions. While some classic tables remain for compatibility, new tables and structures are central to S/4HANA's architecture. Here's a breakdown of commonly used tables, keeping in mind the S/4HANA context:

1. Material Master Data:

  • MARA: General Material Data (basic information like material type, material group, etc.)
  • MAKT: Material Descriptions (descriptions in different languages)
  • MARM: Units of Measure for Material (quantity units, dimensions, etc.)
  • MBEW: Material Valuation (accounting and valuation data)
  • MARD: Storage Location Data for Material (stock quantities per storage location)

2. Purchasing:

  • EKKO: Purchasing Document Header (general information about the purchase order)
  • EKPO: Purchasing Document Item (details of each item in the order)
  • EKET: Scheduling Agreements (delivery schedule dates)
  • EINA: Purchasing Info Record - General Data (general information about the vendor-material relationship)
  • EINE: Purchasing Info Record - Purchasing Organization Data (data specific to the purchasing organization)
  • EBAN: Purchase Requisition (internal request for procurement)

3. Inventory Management (Key Change: MATDOC):

  • MATDOC: This is the most important table for inventory management in S/4HANA. It replaces the classic combination of MKPF (header) and MSEG (item) tables. MATDOC stores all material document information in a single table, significantly improving performance. This is a crucial change in S/4HANA.
    • MKPF and MSEG still exist, primarily for compatibility with older reports and programs. However, for new development and reporting, MATDOC should be used.
  • MBLNR: Material Document Number (still relevant for referencing documents).
  • BWART: Movement Type (identifies the type of stock movement).
  • LGORT: Storage Location.
  • MATNR: Material Number.

4. Valuation:

  • MBEW: Material Valuation (accounting and valuation data, standard price, moving average price, etc.)
  • CKMI: Price History (history of price changes)

5. Material Requirements Planning (MRP):

  • MDKP: MRP Header Data (general information about the MRP run)
  • MDTB: Planning Table (results of the requirements calculation)

6. General and Organizational Data:

  • T001W: Plants
  • T024E: Purchasing Organizations
  • T001L: Storage Locations

7. Pricing Conditions (Important Change: PRCD_ELEMENTS):

  • PRCD_ELEMENTS: This is the central pricing table in S/4HANA. It stores pricing element details (discounts, surcharges, taxes) for various business processes (sales, purchasing, billing). This table improves performance and flexibility compared to older condition tables.
    • KONV, KONP, and condition tables (A001-AXXX) still exist for compatibility but are less central in S/4HANA.

Key S/4HANA Considerations:

  • Simplified Data Model: S/4HANA aims to reduce data redundancy. MATDOC and PRCD_ELEMENTS are prime examples of this simplification.
  • Compatibility Views: SAP provides compatibility views to map old table structures to new ones. This allows older programs to continue working.
  • CDS Views: Core Data Services (CDS) views are heavily used for data access and reporting. They provide a virtual data model on top of the physical tables, simplifying data consumption. This is the preferred way to access data in S/4HANA.
  • Business Partner (BP): While not a table itself, the Business Partner is a key concept in S/4HANA. It replaces vendor master (LFA1, LFB1, etc.) and customer master data, using tables like BUT000.

In summary: While some classic MM tables remain, S/4HANA introduces key changes with tables like MATDOC and PRCD_ELEMENTS, along with the Business Partner concept and CDS views. When working in an S/4HANA system, focus on understanding these new structures and using CDS views for data access whenever possible. This will ensure better performance and alignment with S/4HANA's architecture.

entry.title: Tables générales utilisées par le module MM de SAP
entry.content:
Le module MM (Material Management) de SAP est vaste et utilise de nombreuses tables pour stocker les données relatives à la gestion des articles, des achats, des stocks, etc. Voici une liste des tables générales les plus utilisées dans le module MM, classées par domaine pour plus de clarté :

1. Données de base articles (Material Master):

  • MARA : Données générales articles (informations de base comme le type d'article, le groupe de marchandises, etc.)
  • MAKT : Désignations des articles (descriptions en différentes langues)
  • MARM : Unités de mesure pour l'article (unités de quantité, dimensions, etc.)
  • MBEW : Valorisation article (données comptables et de valorisation)
  • MARD : Données de stockage article par magasin (quantités en stock par emplacement)

2. Achats (Purchasing):

  • EKKO : En-tête de document d'achat (informations générales sur la commande d'achat)
  • EKPO : Poste de document d'achat (détails de chaque position de la commande)
  • EKET : Échéances du programme de livraison (dates de livraison prévues)
  • EINA : Fiche infos-achats - données générales (informations générales sur la relation fournisseur-article)
  • EINE : Fiche infos-ach. - données de l'organisation d'achat (données spécifiques à l'organisation d'achat)
  • EBAN : Demande d'achat (requête interne pour l'approvisionnement)

3. Mouvements de stock (Inventory Management):

  • MKPF : En-tête du document article (informations générales sur le mouvement de stock)
  • MSEG : Segment de document pour article (détails de chaque mouvement de stock)
  • MBLNR : Numéro de document article.
  • BWART : Type de mouvement.
  • LGORT : Emplacement de stockage.
  • MATNR : Numéro d'article.

4. Valorisation (Valuation):

  • MBEW : Valorisation article (données comptables et de valorisation, prix standard, prix moyen mobile, etc.)
  • CKMI : Historique des prix (historique des modifications de prix)

5. Planification des besoins (Material Requirements Planning - MRP):

  • MDKP : Données en-tête document de planification (informations générales sur l'exécution MRP)
  • MDTB : Table de planification (résultats du calcul des besoins)

6. Données générales et organisations:

  • T001W : Tables des usines
  • T024E : Organisations d'achat
  • T001L : Emplacements de stockage

Tables importantes pour les conditions de prix:

  • KONV : Conditions (données des conditions de prix)
  • KONP : Poste de condition (détails de chaque condition)
  • A001 à AXXX : Tables de conditions (dépendent du type de condition)
  • PRCD_ELEMENTS : Table de prix dans S/4HANA (simplifie la structure des données de prix)

Remarques importantes :

  • Cette liste n'est pas exhaustive, mais elle couvre les tables les plus couramment utilisées.
  • Dans S/4HANA, certaines tables ont été remplacées ou fusionnées pour simplifier le modèle de données. Par exemple, la table MATDOC remplace la combinaison de MKPF et MSEG pour les documents articles.
  • L'accès direct aux tables en production est généralement déconseillé. Il est préférable d'utiliser les transactions et les rapports standard de SAP ou de créer des requêtes personnalisées via des vues CDS (Core Data Services) dans S/4HANA.
  • La structure des tables et les noms des champs peuvent varier légèrement en fonction de la version de SAP utilisée.

Pour une recherche plus spécifique, il est utile de connaître la transaction ou le processus métier concerné. Par exemple, si vous recherchez les tables utilisées pour la réception de marchandises, vous pouvez vous concentrer sur les tables MKPF et MSEG.

En utilisant ces informations, vous devriez être en mesure de mieux comprendre la structure des données dans le module MM de SAP. N'hésitez pas à poser d'autres questions si vous avez besoin de précisions.
